cards.front: What are the six categories of control flow statements in C++?
cards.back:
1) Conditional statements (if, switch)
2) Jumps (goto, break, continue)
3) Function calls
4) Loops (while, do-while, for)
5) Halts (std::exit, std::abort)
6) Exceptions (try, throw, catch)

cards.text: Use {{c1::while}} loops when iteration count is unknown. Use {{c2::for}} loops when iteration count is known. Use {{c3::do-while}} when the body must execute at least once.

cards.explanation:
Key practices for if statements:
  • Always use braces { } even for single statements
  • Use == for comparison, not = (assignment)
  • Watch for accidental semicolons after conditions
  • Be aware of the dangling else problem
  • Don't compare floating point with == or !=
  • Prefer if constexpr when condition is compile-time

Key practices for switch statements:
  • Always use break unless fallthrough is intentional
  • Use [[fallthrough]] attribute for intentional fallthrough
  • Place default case last by convention
  • Case labels must be constant expressions
  • Use braces { } when declaring variables in cases
  • Prefer switch over if-else chains for testing integral values

cards.front: What is the recommended usage of goto in modern C++?
cards.back: Avoid goto entirely. Use loops, functions, and other control structures instead. Goto creates hard-to-understand spaghetti code.

cards.front: What is the key difference between break and continue in loops?
cards.back: Break exits the loop entirely. Continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration.

cards.text: The three components every loop needs: {{c1::initialization}}, {{c2::condition}}, and {{c3::update}}. Forgetting the update usually causes an {{c4::infinite loop}}.

cards.concept: Random number generation best practices summary
cards.explanation:
Modern C++ random number generation:
  • Never use rand() and srand() - use <random>
  • Use std::mt19937 (Mersenne Twister) engine
  • Seed with std::random_device for different results
  • Make engine static or global to persist state
  • Use distributions (uniform_int_distribution, etc.)
  • Create a Random namespace with helper functions

cards.front: What is the preferred way to exit a program?
cards.back: Return from main(). This is cleanest and ensures all destructors are called. Avoid std::exit() and std::abort() in most cases.

cards.question: What's the most efficient loop structure for this task: print numbers 1 to 10?
cards.explanation: For loops are best when the iteration count is known. All loop control (init, condition, update) is in one place.

cards.question: When is switch better than if-else?
cards.code: // You need to test one integral variable against multiple constant values
cards.answer: Switch is better when testing a single integral/enum variable against 3+ constant values. It's more readable and potentially more efficient than if-else chains.
cards.explanation: Switch is optimized for this case and makes the intent clearer. If-else is needed for ranges, non-integral types, or complex conditions.

cards.front: When should you use if constexpr instead of regular if?
cards.back: When the condition can be evaluated at compile-time and you want to eliminate dead code. Common in template metaprogramming.

cards.explanation:
Watch out for these frequent bugs:
  • Using = instead of == in conditions
  • Accidental semicolons after if/while/for
  • Forgetting break in switch cases
  • Forgetting to update loop counter (infinite loop)
  • Dangling else without braces
  • Not using braces for single statements
  • Using rand() instead of <random>
examples:
// Bug: no update
while (i < 10) { // infinite loop
    std::cout << i;
}

cards.front: What is the scope of a variable declared in a for loop's init-statement?
cards.back: Loop scope only - the variable exists from declaration until the end of the loop, then is destroyed.

cards.explanation:
Choose the right control structure:
  • if/else: 1-2 conditions, any type, complex expressions
  • switch: Testing one integral variable vs many constants
  • while: Unknown iteration count, may skip entirely
  • do-while: Must execute at least once
  • for: Known iteration count, counter-based
  • goto: Never (use other structures)
examples:
if (complex || condition) { } // use if
switch (menuChoice) { } // use switch
for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { } // use for
